topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink on the paper seemed to bleed into my vision. A C-minus. It wasn't a disaster, not by any stretch, but it felt like a physical blow, a sudden drop in temperature that left my hands clammy. I reread the feedback, each word a tiny shard of criticism catching in my throat. My shoulders immediately tightened, hunching inward as if trying to make myself smaller. I spent the rest of the evening staring out the window, watching the rain streak the glass, the drumming sound mirroring the frantic thumping in my chest. I felt a profound sense of unfairness, as if the world had suddenly tilted, and I was the only one who had slipped off the axis.

[story 2]

"A B-minus," the professor had announced, his voice smooth and utterly devoid of inflection. I clutched the printed grade sheet so tightly my knuckles turned white. When I finally got home, the silence of my room was deafening. I didn't move from the chair; I just sat there, tracing the pattern on the worn wooden desk with my fingertip. A low, involuntary shudder ran through me. It wasn't anger, exactly, but a deep, heavy ache, a feeling of being fundamentally misunderstood. I kept replaying the lecture in my head, searching for the moment I had misread the instructions, as if the answer lay in a misplaced comma.

[story 3]

I stared at the score, my breath catching in shallow gasps. It was lower than I had meticulously calculated, a discrepancy that felt monumental. A wave of heat rushed up my neck, making my skin prickle uncomfortably. I pushed the textbook away, the sound of the paper sliding across the desk sounding excessively loud in the quiet apartment. I started pacing the small space, my movements jerky and restless. Every corner of the room seemed charged with an unspoken disappointment, a heavy atmosphere pressing down on my chest until it felt like I couldn't draw a full breath. I just wanted the feeling to dissipate, to become nothing.
